title: Prompt Engineering — Practical Examples by Subject

scenes.name: Title
scenes.text: Prompt engineering — practical examples by subject. [pause] Same principles. Different classrooms.

scenes.name: Why
scenes.text: Principles only matter when you can apply them. [pause] So let's look at three real examples — math, language, history — and see what changes between weak and strong.

scenes.name: Math weak
scenes.text: Math example. [pause] Weak prompt — <emphasis>make maths exercises for Year 1</emphasis>. [pause] Strong prompt — you are a Year 1 teacher. Create 20 single-digit addition exercises with sums up to 10. Use each digit at least once. No answers.

scenes.name: Math why
scenes.text: What changed. [pause] A role. A precise count. Mathematical constraints. A format. [pause] The AI's capability didn't change. The context did.

scenes.name: Lang weak
scenes.text: Language example. [pause] Weak — <emphasis>make English exercises</emphasis>. [pause] Strong — write an eight-sentence Present Simple story about a morning routine, then create true-false, gap-fill, question-formation, and a short writing task. With answer keys.

scenes.name: Lang why
scenes.text: Notice the pedagogical structure. [pause] Receptive skills first — true-false, gap-fill. Then productive — question formation. Then free writing. [pause] The prompt mirrors how a language teacher actually plans.

scenes.name: History
scenes.text: History example. [pause] Strong prompt — a chronological overview from 1800 to 2000. At least 12 inventions. Inventor, year, country, significance. [pause] Span transport, communication, medicine, computing. [pause] Constraints make it pedagogical.

scenes.name: Patterns
scenes.text: The pattern. [pause] Role. Audience. Specific constraints. Anticipating the final form — answer keys, name fields, rubrics. [pause] The design comes from you. AI handles the production.
